stem: Using Euler's theorem, find the remainder when \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} \) is divided by \( [[n]] \).
solution: Since \( \gcd([[a]], [[n]]) = 1 \), Euler's theorem gives \( [[a]]^{\varphi([[n]])} \equiv 1 \pmod{[[n]]} \). Reduce the exponent \( [[b]] \) modulo \( \varphi([[n]]) \) and evaluate to get \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} \equiv [[answer]] \pmod{[[n]]} \).
distractor_rationales: This uses exponent \( [[b]]+1 \), one power too many.
This uses exponent \( [[b]]-1 \), one power too few.
This is \( [[a]]^{2} \bmod [[n]] \), not the value at exponent \( [[b]] \).
This is \( [[a]] \bmod [[n]] \), the base itself.
This is one more than the correct remainder.
This is two more than the correct remainder.
This is ten more than the correct remainder (a tens-digit slip).

stem: The modulus \( [[n]] = [[p]]^{[[e]]} \) is a prime power. Using Euler's theorem with \( \varphi([[n]]) = [[p]]^{[[e]]-1}([[p]]-1) \), find the remainder when \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} \) is divided by \( [[n]] \).
solution: For the prime power modulus \( [[n]]=[[p]]^{[[e]]} \), Euler's totient is \( \varphi([[n]]) = [[p]]^{[[e]]-1}([[p]]-1) \), so \( [[a]]^{\varphi([[n]])}\equiv 1\pmod{[[n]]} \) (as \( \gcd([[a]],[[p]])=1 \)). Reducing the exponent \( [[b]] \) modulo \( \varphi([[n]]) \) gives \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} \equiv [[answer]] \pmod{[[n]]} \).
distractor_rationales: This uses exponent \( [[b]]+1 \), one power too many.
This uses exponent \( [[b]]-1 \), one power too few.
This reduces \( [[a]] \) mod the prime \( [[p]] \) instead of mod the prime power \( [[n]] \).
This uses the exponent \( [[p]]-1 \), the totient formula for a plain prime modulus, not a prime power.
This is one more than the correct remainder.
This is two more than the correct remainder.
This is off from the correct remainder by \( [[p]] \).

stem: Using Euler's theorem on each factor, find the remainder when \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} \cdot [[c]]^{[[d]]} \) is divided by \( [[n]] \).
solution: Reduce each factor mod \( [[n]] \) using Euler's theorem (\( \varphi([[n]]) \) annihilates both \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} \) and \( [[c]]^{[[d]]} \) after reducing their exponents), then multiply the residues and reduce mod \( [[n]] \) once more: the remainder is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This only reduces the first factor, dropping the second entirely.
This only reduces the second factor, dropping the first entirely.
This adds the two reduced factors instead of multiplying them.
This uses one extra power of the second factor.
This is one more than the correct remainder.
This is two more than the correct remainder.
This is ten more than the correct remainder (a tens-digit slip).

stem: Using Euler's theorem on each term, find the remainder when \( [[a]]^{[[b]]} + [[c]]^{[[d]]} \) is divided by \( [[n]] \).
solution: Reduce each term separately by Euler's theorem (\( [[a]]^{\varphi([[n]])}\equiv1 \) and \( [[c]]^{\varphi([[n]])}\equiv1\pmod{[[n]]} \)), then add the residues and reduce mod \( [[n]] \) once more: the remainder is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This only reduces the first term, dropping the second entirely.
This only reduces the second term, dropping the first entirely.
This multiplies the two reduced terms instead of adding them.
This is one more than the correct remainder.
This is two more than the correct remainder.
This is ten more than the correct remainder (a tens-digit slip).
This uses one extra power on the second term.

stem: Using Euler's theorem, find the remainder when \( [[a]]^{[[k]]!} \) is divided by \( [[n]] \).
solution: First compute the exponent itself: \( [[k]]! \). By Euler's theorem \( [[a]]^{\varphi([[n]])}\equiv 1\pmod{[[n]]} \), so only \( [[k]]! \bmod \varphi([[n]]) \) matters; evaluating gives \( [[a]]^{[[k]]!} \equiv [[answer]] \pmod{[[n]]} \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the un-evaluated exponent \( [[k]] \) instead of \( [[k]]! \).
This is \( [[a]] \bmod [[n]] \), as if the exponent reduced to 1.
This reduces the exponent \( [[k]]! \) itself mod \( [[n]] \) instead of using it as a power of \( [[a]] \).
This is one more than the correct remainder.
This is two more than the correct remainder.
This is ten more than the correct remainder (a tens-digit slip).
This is \( [[a]]^{2} \bmod [[n]] \), as if the exponent reduced to 2.
